parts.content: Solve the differential equation, giving your answer in the form $g(u, v) = 0$.
parts.markscheme:
• Recognize homogeneous differential equation; substitute $v = yu$ or use $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = y + u\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}u}$ M1
  • Substitute into DE: $$y + u\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}u} = \frac{yu-u}{yu+u} = \frac{y-1}{y+1}$$ A1
  • Rearrange and separate variables: $$u\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}u} = \frac{y-1}{y+1} - y = \frac{y-1-y^2-y}{y+1} = \frac{-(y^2+1)}{y+1}$$ $$\int \frac{y+1}{y^2+1} \, \mathrm{d}y = \int -\frac{1}{u} \, \mathrm{d}u$$ M1
  • Integrate LHS: $$\int \frac{y}{y^2+1} \, \mathrm{d}y + \int \frac{1}{y^2+1} \, \mathrm{d}y = \frac{1}{2}\ln(y^2+1) + \arctan(y)$$ A1
  • Integrate RHS and form equation: $$\frac{1}{2}\ln(y^2+1) + \arctan(y) = -\ln u + C$$ A1
  • Substitute $y = \frac{v}{u}$: $$\frac{1}{2}\ln\left(\frac{v^2}{u^2}+1\right) + \arctan\left(\frac{v}{u}\right) = -\ln u + C$$ M1
  • Simplify logarithmic terms: $$\frac{1}{2}\ln\left(\frac{v^2+u^2}{u^2}\right) + \arctan\left(\frac{v}{u}\right) = -\ln u + C$$ $$\frac{1}{2}\ln(v^2+u^2) - \ln u + \arctan\left(\frac{v}{u}\right) = -\ln u + C$$ $$\frac{1}{2}\ln(v^2+u^2) + \arctan\left(\frac{v}{u}\right) = C$$ A1
  • Apply initial condition $u=1, v=2$: $$\frac{1}{2}\ln(2^2+1^2) + \arctan\left(\frac{2}{1}\right) = C$$ $$C = \frac{1}{2}\ln 5 + \arctan 2$$ M1
  • State final answer in the form $g(u,v)=0$: $$\frac{1}{2}\ln(v^2+u^2) + \arctan\left(\frac{v}{u}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\ln 5 - \arctan 2 = 0$$ A1
9 marks total

steps.title: Identify the type of Differential Equation
steps.content:
First, we observe that the differential equation is **homogeneous**. This is because the function on the right-hand side, $f(u, v) = \frac{v-u}{v+u}$, remains unchanged if we replace $v$ with $kv$ and $u$ with $ku$:

$$f(ku, kv) = \frac{kv-ku}{kv+ku} = \frac{k(v-u)}{k(v+u)} = \frac{v-u}{v+u}$$

steps.title: Perform variable substitution
steps.content:
To solve a homogeneous differential equation, we use the substitution $v = yu$, where $y$ is a function of $u$. Using the product rule to differentiate $v$ with respect to $u$:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = y + u\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}u}$$

highlights.text: Substitute $v = yu$ or use $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = y + u\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}u}$

steps.title: Substitute into the equation
steps.content:
Now we substitute our expressions for $v$ and $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}$ into the original equation:

$$y + u\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}u} = \frac{yu - u}{yu + u}$$

Factor out $u$ on the right-hand side to simplify:

$$y + u\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}u} = \frac{u(y - 1)}{u(y + 1)} = \frac{y - 1}{y + 1}$$

steps.title: Separate the variables
steps.content:
We need to separate the variables $y$ and $u$. First, subtract $y$ from both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} u\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}u} &= \frac{y - 1}{y + 1} - y \\ &= \frac{y - 1 - y(y + 1)}{y + 1} \\ &= \frac{y - 1 - y^2 - y}{y + 1} \\ &= \frac{-(y^2 + 1)}{y + 1} \end{aligned}$$

Now, rearrange to group $y$ terms with $\mathrm{d}y$ and $u$ terms with $\mathrm{d}u$:

$$\frac{y + 1}{y^2 + 1} \, \mathrm{d}y = -\frac{1}{u} \, \mathrm{d}u$$

steps.title: Integrate both sides
steps.content:
Integrate both sides. For the left side, we split the fraction into two standard integrals:

$$\int \frac{y}{y^2 + 1} \, \mathrm{d}y + \int \frac{1}{y^2 + 1} \, \mathrm{d}y = \int -\frac{1}{u} \, \mathrm{d}u$$

steps.title: Evaluate the integrals
steps.content:
We evaluate each term:
1.  **First term:** $\int \frac{y}{y^2+1} \, \mathrm{d}y$. The numerator is half the derivative of the denominator ($2y$). This integrates to $\frac{1}{2}\ln(y^2+1)$.
2.  **Second term:** $\int \frac{1}{y^2+1} \, \mathrm{d}y$. This is the standard integral for $\arctan(y)$.
3.  **Right side:** $\int -\frac{1}{u} \, \mathrm{d}u = -\ln u + C$.

Putting it all together:

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln(y^2 + 1) + \arctan(y) = -\ln u + C$$

steps.title: Substitute back and simplify
steps.content:
Recall that $v = yu$, so substitute $y = \frac{v}{u}$ back into the equation:

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln\left(\left(\frac{v}{u}\right)^2 + 1\right) + \arctan\left(\frac{v}{u}\right) = -\ln u + C$$

Simplify the logarithmic term:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2}\ln\left(\frac{v^2 + u^2}{u^2}\right) &= \frac{1}{2}\left[\ln(v^2 + u^2) - \ln(u^2)\right] \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\ln(v^2 + u^2) - \frac{1}{2}(2\ln u) \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\ln(v^2 + u^2) - \ln u \end{aligned}$$

Notice that $-\ln u$ appears on both sides of the equation and cancels out:

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln(v^2 + u^2) - \ln u + \arctan\left(\frac{v}{u}\right) = -\ln u + C$$

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln(v^2 + u^2) + \arctan\left(\frac{v}{u}\right) = C$$

steps.title: Find the constant C
steps.content:
Use the initial condition given in the question: $v = 2$ when $u = 1$.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2}\ln(2^2 + 1^2) + \arctan\left(\frac{2}{1}\right) &= C \\ \frac{1}{2}\ln(5) + \arctan(2) &= C \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the value of C to verify your work numerically.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Substitute the value of $C$ back into the general solution and rearrange to the form $g(u, v) = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln(v^2 + u^2) + \arctan\left(\frac{v}{u}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\ln 5 - \arctan 2 = 0$$

parts.content: The graph of $$v$$ against $$u$$ has a local maximum between $$u=2$$ and $$u=3$$. Determine the coordinates of this local maximum.
parts.markscheme:
• Recognize local maximum implies $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = 0$ M1
  • Set $\frac{v-u}{v+u} = 0 \Rightarrow v = u$ A1
  • Substitute $v=u$ into the solution from Part 1: $$\frac{1}{2}\ln(u^2+u^2) + \arctan\left(\frac{u}{u}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\ln 5 + \arctan 2$$ $$\frac{1}{2}\ln(2u^2) + \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{1}{2}\ln 5 + \arctan 2$$ M1
  • Solve for $u$: $$\ln(2u^2) = \ln 5 + 2\arctan 2 - \frac{\pi}{2}$$ $$2u^2 = 5e^{2\arctan 2 - \frac{\pi}{2}}$$ $$u \approx 2.18$$ A1
  • The coordinates of the local maximum are $(2.18, 2.18)$.
4 marks total

steps.title: Identify condition for local maximum
steps.content:
To find the coordinates of a local maximum, we look for where the derivative of the function is zero. In this case, we need to set $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = 0$.

From the differential equation given in the question:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = \frac{v - u}{v + u}$$

steps.title: Solve for the relationship between v and u
steps.content:
Set the derivative expression to zero and solve for the relationship between $v$ and $u$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{v - u}{v + u} &= 0 \\ v - u &= 0 \\ v &= u \end{aligned}$$

This tells us that at the local maximum, the coordinate values $v$ and $u$ must be equal.

steps.title: Recall the general solution from Part 1
steps.content:
We use the implicit general solution derived in the previous part of the question. Based on the differential equation and the initial condition ($v=2$ when $u=1$), the solution is:

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln(u^2+v^2) + \arctan\left(\frac{v}{u}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\ln 5 + \arctan 2$$

This equation relates $u$ and $v$ for all points on the curve.

steps.title: Substitute v = u into the equation
steps.content:
Now we substitute the condition $v = u$ into our general solution to find the specific value of $u$ at the maximum.

Substitute $v$ with $u$:

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln(u^2+u^2) + \arctan\left(\frac{u}{u}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\ln 5 + \arctan 2$$

Simplify the terms inside the functions:

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln(2u^2) + \arctan(1) = \frac{1}{2}\ln 5 + \arctan 2$$

steps.title: Simplify using exact values
steps.content:
We know from our exact trigonometric values that $\arctan(1) = \frac{\pi}{4}$.

Substitute this into the equation:

$$\frac{1}{2}\ln(2u^2) + \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{1}{2}\ln 5 + \arctan 2$$

To isolate $u$, let's multiply the entire equation by $2$ to remove the fractions:

$$\ln(2u^2) + \frac{\pi}{2} = \ln 5 + 2\arctan 2$$

steps.title: Solve for u
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to solve for $u$:

$$\begin{aligned} \ln(2u^2) &= \ln 5 + 2\arctan 2 - \frac{\pi}{2} \\ 2u^2 &= e^{\ln 5 + 2\arctan 2 - \frac{\pi}{2}} \\ 2u^2 &= 5e^{2\arctan 2 - \frac{\pi}{2}} \\ u^2 &= \frac{5}{2}e^{2\arctan 2 - \frac{\pi}{2}} \\ u &= \sqrt{\frac{5}{2}e^{2\arctan 2 - \frac{\pi}{2}}} \end{aligned}$$

Note: We take the positive root because the question specifies $u > 0$.

steps.title: Calculate the numerical value
steps.content:
Finally, evaluate the expression using a calculator.

$$u \approx \sqrt{2.5 \cdot e^{2(1.107) - 1.571}} \approx 2.18$$

Since $v = u$, the coordinate $v$ is also $2.18$.

The coordinates of the local maximum are $(2.18, 2.18)$.

To find the local maximum of $v$ against $u$, we look for where the derivative $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}$ is equal to zero.

From Part 1 (as referenced in the markscheme), the implicit general solution to the differential equation is:

We can now solve for $u$ by plotting the left-hand side and right-hand side as separate functions on your GDC and finding their intersection. The intersection represents the peak of the curve where $v=u$.

parts.content: Show that there are no points of inflexion on the graph of $$v$$ against $$u$$.
parts.markscheme:
Method 1
  • Attempt to find second derivative (quotient rule): $$\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} = \frac{(v+u)(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}-1) - (v-u)(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}+1)}{(v+u)^2}$$ M1
  • Substitute $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = \frac{v-u}{v+u}$ and simplify numerator: Numerator $= (v+u)\left(\frac{v-u}{v+u}-1\right) - (v-u)\left(\frac{v-u}{v+u}+1\right)$ $= (v-u - (v+u)) - (v-u)\frac{v-u+v+u}{v+u}$ $= -2u - \frac{2v(v-u)}{v+u} = \frac{-2u(v+u)-2v(v-u)}{v+u}$ $= \frac{-2uv-2u^2-2v^2+2uv}{v+u} = \frac{-2(u^2+v^2)}{v+u}$ A1
  • Obtain expression for second derivative: $$\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} = \frac{-2(u^2+v^2)}{(v+u)^3}$$ A1
  • Reasoning: Since $u,v > 0$, numerator is negative and denominator is positive. Therefore $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} \neq 0$ (always negative), so no points of inflexion. R1
Method 2
  • Rearrange to $(v+u)\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = v-u$ and attempt implicit differentiation w.r.t $u$ M1
  • $$(v+u)\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} + \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}+1\right)\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}-1$$ A1
  • Simplify: $$(v+u)\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} = \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} - 1 - \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}\right)^2 - \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = -1 - \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}\right)^2$$ A1
  • Reasoning: RHS is always negative (since squared term is non-negative). Therefore $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} \neq 0$, so no points of inflexion. R1
4 marks total

steps.title: Recall condition for inflexion
steps.content:
To find points of inflexion, we need to find the second derivative $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2}$ and check where it equals zero or changes sign.

Our goal is to calculate $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2}$ and show that it can never be zero for $u, v > 0$.

steps.title: Apply the quotient rule
steps.content:
We differentiate the given expression $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = \frac{v - u}{v + u}$ with respect to $u$ using the quotient rule $\left(\frac{f}{g}\right)' = \frac{gf' - fg'}{g^2}$.

Here, the numerator is $(v-u)$ and the denominator is $(v+u)$. Remember that $v$ is a function of $u$, so the derivative of $v$ is $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}$.

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} = \frac{(v+u)\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}u}(v-u) - (v-u)\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}u}(v+u)}{(v+u)^2}$$

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} = \frac{(v+u)(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} - 1) - (v-u)(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} + 1)}{(v+u)^2}$$

steps.title: Substitute the first derivative
steps.content:
We substitute the original expression $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = \frac{v - u}{v + u}$ into our equation for the second derivative. Let's focus on simplifying the numerator first.

$$\text{Numerator} = (v+u)\left(\frac{v-u}{v+u} - 1\right) - (v-u)\left(\frac{v-u}{v+u} + 1\right)$$

steps.title: Simplify the numerator terms
steps.content:
Let's simplify the two parts of the numerator separately:

**First part:**
$$(v+u)\left(\frac{v-u}{v+u} - 1\right) = (v-u) - (v+u) = -2u$$

**Second part:**
To simplify $(v-u)(\frac{v-u}{v+u} + 1)$, we first combine the terms inside the bracket:
$$\frac{v-u}{v+u} + 1 = \frac{v-u + (v+u)}{v+u} = \frac{2v}{v+u}$$
So the second part becomes:
$$(v-u)\left(\frac{2v}{v+u}\right) = \frac{2v(v-u)}{v+u}$$

steps.title: Combine and fully simplify
steps.content:
Now we combine these parts back into the numerator expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &= -2u - \frac{2v(v-u)}{v+u} \\ &= \frac{-2u(v+u) - 2v(v-u)}{v+u} \\ &= \frac{-2uv - 2u^2 - 2v^2 + 2uv}{v+u} \\ &= \frac{-2u^2 - 2v^2}{v+u} \\ &= \frac{-2(u^2+v^2)}{v+u} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Write final second derivative
steps.content:
Place the simplified numerator back over the original denominator $(v+u)^2$:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} = \frac{\frac{-2(u^2+v^2)}{v+u}}{(v+u)^2} = \frac{-2(u^2+v^2)}{(v+u)^3}$$

steps.title: Determine the nature of the derivative
steps.content:
The question states that $u, v > 0$. We analyze the sign of our expression:

1.  $u^2 + v^2$ must be positive.
2.  $(v+u)^3$ must be positive.
3.  The numerator $-2(u^2+v^2)$ is therefore always **negative**.

Since the result is a negative number divided by a positive number, $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2}$ is always negative and can never be zero. Therefore, there are **no points of inflexion**.

steps.title: State condition for inflexion
steps.content:
A point of inflexion occurs where the concavity of the function changes. A necessary condition for this is that the second derivative is zero:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} = 0$$

To check this, we need to find an expression for the second derivative.

steps.title: Rearrange the equation
steps.content:
Using the **Rearrangement & Implicit Differentiation** method, we first eliminate the fraction by multiplying both sides by the denominator $(v+u)$:

$$(v+u)\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = v - u$$

steps.title: Differentiate implicitly
steps.content:
Now we differentiate both sides with respect to $u$. For the left-hand side, we apply the product rule $(uv)' = u'v + uv'$.

- **LHS:** The derivative of $(v+u)$ is $\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} + 1\right)$, and the derivative of $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}$ is $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2}$.
- **RHS:** The derivative of $(v - u)$ is $\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} - 1\right)$.

Applying this gives:

$$(v+u)\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} + \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} + 1\right)\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} - 1$$

steps.title: Simplify the equation
steps.content:
Expand the terms on the left side to simplify the expression:

$$(v+u)\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} + \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}\right)^2 + \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} = \frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u} - 1$$

Subtract $\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}$ from both sides to cancel that term:

$$(v+u)\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} + \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}\right)^2 = -1$$

steps.title: Isolate the second derivative term
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to isolate the term with the second derivative:

$$(v+u)\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} = -1 - \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}\right)^2$$

$$(v+u)\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} = -\left[1 + \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}\right)^2\right]$$

steps.title: Analyze signs for inflexion points
steps.content:
To have a point of inflexion, we need $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2} = 0$. Let's examine the signs of each part of our equation:

1.  **RHS:** The term $1 + \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}v}{\mathrm{d}u}\right)^2$ is always positive (specifically $\ge 1$) because a square is non-negative. Therefore, the entire right-hand side is always negative ($\le -1$).
2.  **LHS:** We are given that $u, v > 0$, so the term $(v+u)$ is always positive.

Since a positive number times $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2}$ equals a negative number, $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2v}{\mathrm{d}u^2}$ must be negative and can never be zero.

Therefore, there are **no points of inflexion**.

$$

with the initial condition $y=1$ when $x=1$.

parts.content: Use the substitution $y=v x$ to solve the differential equation, giving your answer in the form $f(x, y)=0$.
parts.markscheme:
Using $y=v x$, so $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=v+x \frac{\mathrm{~d} v}{\mathrm{~d} x}$.\

Substitute into the differential equation:

$$
v+x \frac{\mathrm{~d} v}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{x^{2}+2 x \cdot v x}{x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}=\frac{1+2 v}{1+v^{2}} .(A 1)
$$

Rearrange:

$$
x \frac{\mathrm{~d} v}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1+2 v-v-v^{3}}{1+v^{2}}=\frac{1+v-v^{3}}{1+v^{2}}
$$

Separate variables:

$$
\int \frac{1+v^{2}}{1+v-v^{3}} \mathrm{~d} v=\int \frac{1}{x} \mathrm{~d} x \cdot(M 1)
$$

Factorize denominator: $1+v-v^{3}=-(v-1)\left(v^{2}+v+1\right)$.
Partial fractions:

$$
\frac{1+v^{2}}{(v-1)\left(v^{2}+v+1\right)}=\frac{A}{v-1}+\frac{B v+C}{v^{2}+v+1} .
$$

Solve: $A=-\frac{1}{3}, B=\frac{1}{3}, C=\frac{2}{3}$.
Integrate:

$$
-\frac{1}{3} \ln |v-1|+\frac{1}{6} \ln \left(v^{2}+v+1\right)+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \arctan \left(\frac{2 v+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=\ln x+C .
$$

Use $x=1, y=1$, so $v=1: C=0$.
Solution:

$$
-\frac{1}{3} \ln \left|\frac{y}{x}-1\right|+\frac{1}{6} \ln \left(\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}+1\right)+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \arctan \left(\frac{2 \frac{y}{x}+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=\ln x .(A 1)
$$

[8 marks]

parts.content: Find the coordinates of the point on the solution curve where the gradient is zero, for $x>1$.
parts.markscheme:
Set $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0$ :

$$
\frac{x^{2}+2 x y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}=0 \Longrightarrow x^{2}+2 x y=0 \Longrightarrow y=-\frac{x}{2} .(M 1)(A 1)
$$

Since $y>0$, no solutions exist for real $x, y>0$.
[4 marks]

questions.specification:
A large reservoir initially contains pure water. Water containing salt begins to flow into the reservoir. The solution is kept uniform by stirring and leaves the reservoir through an outlet at its base. Let $x$ grams represent the amount of salt in the reservoir and let $t$ minutes represent the time since the salt water began flowing into the reservoir.

The rate of change of the amount of salt in the reservoir, $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$, is described by the differential equation $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1}$.

parts.content: Show that $t + 1$ is an integrating factor for this differential equation.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

- Integrating factor formula: $I(t) = \text{e}^{\int P(t) \,\text{d}t}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $\text{e}^{\int \frac{1}{t + 1} \,\text{d}t}$

- $= \text{e}^{\ln(t + 1)}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- $= t + 1$

### Method #2

- Attempt product rule differentiation on $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(x(t + 1))$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(x(t + 1)) = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}(t + 1) + x$

- $= (t + 1)(\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{t + 1})$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Therefore, $t + 1$ is an integrating factor for this differential equation

<marks>2 marks total</marks>

steps.title: Rearrange into standard form
steps.content:
To find the integrating factor, we first write the differential equation in the standard linear form:
$$ \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + P(t)x = Q(t) $$

The given equation is:
$$ \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1} $$

Add $\frac{x}{t+1}$ to both sides to group the $x$ terms:
$$ \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{1}{t+1}x = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} $$

steps.title: Identify the function P(t)
steps.content:
Comparing our rearranged equation to the standard form $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + P(t)x = Q(t)$, we identify the coefficient of $x$:
$$ P(t) = \frac{1}{t+1} $$

steps.title: Apply integrating factor formula
steps.content:
The formula for the integrating factor $I(t)$ is:
$$ I(t) = \text{e}^{\int P(t) \,\text{d}t} $$

Substituting our function for $P(t)$:
$$ I(t) = \text{e}^{\int \frac{1}{t+1} \,\text{d}t} $$

steps.title: Evaluate the integral
steps.content:
We evaluate the integral in the exponent. Using the standard integral (derived from the derivative of $\ln x$ in the data booklet):
$$ \int \frac{1}{t+1} \,\text{d}t = \ln(t+1) $$

So the integrating factor becomes:
$$ I(t) = \text{e}^{\ln(t+1)} $$

Note: We assume $t+1 > 0$ since $t$ represents time, so absolute value signs are not required.

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Using the logarithmic property that exponentials and natural logarithms are inverse functions ($\text{e}^{\ln u} = u$):
$$ I(t) = t+1 $$

Thus, we have shown that $t+1$ is the integrating factor.

steps.title: Differentiate the product
steps.content:
To verify the integrating factor, we start by differentiating the product $x(t + 1)$ with respect to $t$ using the product rule.

Using the rule $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(uv) = u\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} + v\frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}t}$ with $u=x$ and $v=(t+1)$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(x(t + 1)) &= x \cdot \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(t + 1) + (t + 1) \cdot \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \\ &= x(1) + (t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \\ &= x + (t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange the differential equation
steps.content:
Next, we rearrange the given differential equation to group the $x$ terms on the left-hand side.

Starting with:
$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1}$$

Add $\frac{x}{t + 1}$ to both sides:
$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{t + 1} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$$

steps.title: Multiply by the factor
steps.content:
Now, multiply the entire rearranged equation by the factor $(t + 1)$ to see if it matches our product derivative.

$$(t + 1)\left[\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{t + 1}\right] = (t + 1)\left[10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}\right]$$

Distributing $(t + 1)$ on the left side:
$$(t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$$ 

Notice that the left-hand side is exactly $x + (t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$.

steps.title: Verify and conclude
steps.content:
Comparing the results from Step 0 and Step 2:

1. Derivative of product: $$x + (t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$$
2. Modified differential equation: $$(t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x$$

Since multiplying the differential equation by $(t + 1)$ transforms the left-hand side into the exact derivative of $x(t + 1)$, we have shown that $t + 1$ is an integrating factor.

parts.content: Hence, by solving this differential equation, show that $x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempting to multiply through by $(t + 1)$ and rearrange <marks>M1</marks>

- $(t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(x(t + 1)\right) = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$

- $x(t + 1) = \int{10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}}\,\text{d}t$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Attempting to integrate the RHS by parts <marks>M1</marks>

- $= -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 40\int{\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}}\,\text{d}t$

- $= -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + C$ <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Condone the absence of $C$.</note>

### Method #1

- Substituting $t = 0, x = 0 \Rightarrow C = 200$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $x = \frac{-40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 200}{t + 1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Using $-40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ as the highest common factor of $-40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ and $-160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

### Method #2

- Using $-40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ as the highest common factor of $-40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ and $-160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ giving

  $x(t + 1) = -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) + C$ <marks>M1A1</marks>

- Substituting $t = 0, x = 0 \Rightarrow C = 200$ <marks>M1</marks>

### Final step for both methods

- $x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1}$ (Answer Given)

<marks>8 marks total</marks>

steps.title: Rewrite in linear form
steps.content:
First, we identify that this is a first-order linear differential equation of the form $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + P(t)x = Q(t)$.

We rearrange the given equation by moving the term with $x$ to the left-hand side:

$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{1}{t + 1}x = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$$ 

Here, we can identify $P(t) = \frac{1}{t+1}$ and $Q(t) = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$.

steps.title: Calculate integrating factor
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the integrating factor, $I(t)$, using the formula $I(t) = \text{e}^{\int P(t) \text{d}t}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Integrating Factor} &= \text{e}^{\int \frac{1}{t + 1} \,\text{d}t} \\ &= \text{e}^{\ln(t + 1)} \\ &= t + 1 \end{aligned}$$

(Note: Since $t$ represents time, $t \ge 0$, so $t+1 > 0$ and we do not need absolute value signs).

steps.title: Multiply by integrating factor
steps.content:
We multiply the entire differential equation by the integrating factor $(t + 1)$:

$$(t + 1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$$

We recognize that the left-hand side is the derivative of the product $x(t+1)$. We can rewrite the equation as:

$$\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left[x(t + 1)\right] = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$$ 

Integrating both sides with respect to $t$ gives:

$$x(t + 1) = \int 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t$$

steps.title: Integrate by parts
steps.content:
To solve the integral on the right-hand side, we use **integration by parts**. The formula is $\int u \frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}x} \text{d}x = uv - \int v \frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}x} \text{d}x$.

Let:
$$\begin{aligned} u &= 10(t + 1) & \frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} &= \text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \\ \frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}t} &= 10 & v &= -4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \end{aligned}$$

Applying the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \int 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t &= [10(t + 1)][-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}] - \int (-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}})(10) \,\text{d}t \\ &= -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 40\int \text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t \\ &= -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 40(-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}) + C \\ &= -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + C \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: General solution
steps.content:
Now we equate our integrated result back to the left-hand side from Step 2:

$$x(t + 1) = -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + C$$

To simplify the right-hand side, we can factor out $-40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ from the first two terms:

$$\begin{aligned} -40(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} &= -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}[(t + 1) + 4] \\ &= -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) \end{aligned}$$

So the general solution is:
$$x(t + 1) = -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) + C$$

steps.title: Find constant C
steps.content:
We use the initial condition to find $C$. The reservoir initially contains pure water, meaning when $t = 0$, the amount of salt $x = 0$.

Substituting $t = 0$ and $x = 0$ into our equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 0(0 + 1) &= -40\text{e}^{0}(0 + 5) + C \\ 0 &= -40(1)(5) + C \\ 0 &= -200 + C \\ C &= 200 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute $C = 200$ back into the general solution and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x(t + 1) &= -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) + 200 \\ x(t + 1) &= 200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) \end{aligned}$$

Dividing by $(t + 1)$ gives the required expression:

$$x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1}$$

steps.title: Analyze the problem
steps.content:
The problem asks us to solve a first-order differential equation to find an expression for the amount of salt $x$ at time $t$. 

We are given:
1. The differential equation: $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1}$
2. Initial conditions: The reservoir initially contains pure water, meaning there is no salt at the start.

This involves concepts from **AHL 5.18—1st order DE’s**.

steps.title: Rearrange and clear denominator
steps.content:
First, we rearrange the equation to group the $x$ and $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ terms on the left side:

$$ \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{t + 1} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} $$

To apply the **Reverse Product Rule by Inspection**, we clear the denominator by multiplying the entire equation by $(t+1)$:

$$ (t+1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x = 10(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} $$

steps.title: Recognize the derivative
steps.content:
By inspecting the left-hand side, $(t+1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + x$, we can recognize this pattern as the derivative of the product $x(t+1)$.

Using the Product Rule $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}(uv) = u\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} + v\frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}t}$ with $u=x$ and $v=t+1$:

$$ \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\big(x(t+1)\big) = x(1) + (t+1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} $$

This matches our equation exactly. We can now rewrite the differential equation as:

$$ \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\big(x(t + 1)\big) = 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} $$

steps.title: Integrate both sides
steps.content:
Now we integrate both sides with respect to $t$. The left side simplifies directly:

$$ x(t + 1) = \int 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t $$

steps.title: Apply Integration by Parts
steps.content:
To solve the integral on the right, we use **Integration by Parts** (**AHL 5.16**). The formula is:

$$ \int u \frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}x} \text{d}x = uv - \int v \frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}x} \text{d}x $$

Let:
$$\begin{aligned} u &= 10(t+1) & \frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} &= \text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \\ \frac{\text{d}u}{\text{d}t} &= 10 & v &= -4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \end{aligned}$$

Applying the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \int 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t &= 10(t+1)(-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}) - \int (-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}})(10) \,\text{d}t \\ &= -40(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 40 \int \text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve the integral
steps.content:
Completing the integration:

$$\begin{aligned} \int 10(t + 1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t &= -40(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + 40(-4\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}) + C \\ &= -40(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + C \end{aligned}$$

So our general solution is:

$$ x(t + 1) = -40(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + C $$

steps.title: Find the constant C
steps.content:
We use the initial condition to find $C$. The problem states the reservoir initially contains pure water, so when $t = 0$, $x = 0$.

Substituting these values:

$$\begin{aligned} 0(0 + 1) &= -40(0+1)\text{e}^{0} - 160\text{e}^{0} + C \\ 0 &= -40(1)(1) - 160(1) + C \\ 0 &= -40 - 160 + C \\ 0 &= -200 + C \\ C &= 200 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate C by evaluating the RHS at t=0

steps.title: Format the final answer
steps.content:
Now we substitute $C = 200$ back into the equation and solve for $x$:

$$ x(t + 1) = 200 - 40(t+1)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - 160\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} $$

Factor out $-40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}$ from the last two terms to simplify:

$$\begin{aligned} x(t + 1) &= 200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \big((t+1) + 4\big) \\ x(t + 1) &= 200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5) \end{aligned}$$

Finally, divide by $(t + 1)$ to get the required form:

$$ x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1} $$

parts.content: Find the value of $t$ at which the amount of salt in the reservoir is decreasing most rapidly.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that we need to find the minimum value of $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Set up the equation for the second derivative:

  $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = -\frac{5}{2}\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} + \frac{x}{(t+1)^2} - \frac{1}{t+1}\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$

- Set $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 0$ and solve for $t$

- Or, graph $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ and find the minimum point

- The amount of salt is decreasing most rapidly at $t = 12.9$ minutes <marks>A1</marks>

<marks>2 marks total</marks>

steps.title: Identify the optimization condition
steps.content:
To find the time when the amount of salt is **decreasing most rapidly**, we need to find the minimum value of the rate of change $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$.

In calculus, finding a minimum or maximum involves finding where the derivative is zero. Therefore, we must find where the second derivative is zero:

$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 0$$

steps.title: Differentiate the rate equation
steps.content:
We are given the differential equation:

$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1}$$

We differentiate this with respect to $t$ to find an expression for $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$. Using the chain rule for the first term and the quotient rule for the second term (where $x$ is a function of $t$):

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} &= \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}\right) - \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(\frac{x}{t+1}\right) \\ &= 10\left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \left(\frac{(t+1)\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} - x(1)}{(t+1)^2}\right) \\ &= -\frac{5}{2}\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{1}{t+1}\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{(t+1)^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up the equation to solve
steps.content:
To find the minimum rate, we set the second derivative to zero:

$$-\frac{5}{2}\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{1}{t+1}\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{(t+1)^2} = 0$$

To solve this for $t$, we need the expression for $x$ in terms of $t$. From the earlier parts of the question (solving the linear differential equation with initial condition $x(0)=0$), the expression for $x$ is:

$$x = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t+5)}{t+1}$$

steps.title: Solve for t using a calculator
steps.content:
We can now solve for $t$ by graphing the derivative of the rate function, or by using the equation solver on a GDC. We are looking for the root of the second derivative function.

The amount of salt is decreasing most rapidly at:

$$t = 12.9 \text{ minutes}$$

steps.title: Interpret the question
steps.content:
The question asks for the time when the amount of salt is **decreasing most rapidly**. 

Since $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ represents the rate of change of salt:
- A negative value of $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ indicates the amount is decreasing.
- "Most rapidly" means we are looking for the **minimum value** (the most negative value) of this rate.

Therefore, we need to find the minimum point of the function $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$.

steps.title: Retrieve the expression for x(t)
steps.content:
To graph the rate $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ on a calculator, we need it purely in terms of $t$. The given equation contains $x$:

$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} - \frac{x}{t + 1}$$

This implies we must use the solution for $x(t)$ found in previous parts (Part 1 or 2). Solving the linear differential equation with the initial condition $x(0)=0$ yields:

$$x(t) = \frac{200 - 40(t+5)\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}}{t+1}$$

steps.title: Define the function to graph
steps.content:
Substitute the expression for $x(t)$ into the rate equation to create a single function of $t$ that we can graph:

$$f(t) = 10\text{e}^{-0.25t} - \frac{\left(\frac{200 - 40(t+5)\text{e}^{-0.25t}}{t+1}\right)}{t + 1}$$

We will plot this function and find its minimum.

3. Plot f2(x) and adjust the window to view the minimum.

2. Define rate(t) = 10*exp(-0.25*t) - x_salt(t)/(t+1)
3. Graph rate(t) and click the curve to find the minimum point.

parts.content:
The rate of change of the amount of salt leaving the reservoir is equal to $\frac{x}{t + 1}$.

Find the amount of salt that left the reservoir during the first 60 minutes.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

- Forming an integral representing the amount of salt that left the reservoir: <marks>M1</marks>

  $\int_{0}^{60} \frac{x(t)}{t + 1} \,\text{d}t$

- Correct substitution of $x(t)$: <marks>A1</marks>

  $\int_{0}^{60} \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}\left( t + 5 \right)}{\left( t + 1 \right)^2} \,\text{d}t$

- Final answer: 36.7 (grams) <marks>A2</marks>

### Method #2

- Forming an integral representing the amount of salt that entered the reservoir minus the amount of salt in the reservoir at $t = 60$ (minutes): <marks>A1</marks>

  $\int_{0}^{60} 10 \text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}\,\text{d}t - x(60)$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Final answer: 36.7 (grams) <marks>A2</marks>

<marks>4 marks total</marks>

steps.title: Identify the output rate
steps.content:
To find the total amount of salt leaving the reservoir, we first identify the rate at which salt leaves. The question explicitly gives this rate.

Rate of salt leaving = $$\frac{x}{t + 1}$$

highlights.text: The rate of change of the amount of salt leaving the reservoir is equal to $\frac{x}{t + 1}$

steps.title: Set up the definite integral
steps.content:
The total amount of salt that left is the integral of the leaving rate over the time interval from $t = 0$ to $t = 60$. We set up the definite integral:

$$ \text{Amount left} = \int_{0}^{60} \frac{x(t)}{t + 1} \,\text{d}t $$

steps.title: Substitute the expression for x(t)
steps.content:
From the previous part of the question (solving the differential equation), the expression for the amount of salt $x(t)$ is:

$$ x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1} $$

Substitute this expression into the integral. Note that dividing by $(t+1)$ again will square the denominator:

$$ \int_{0}^{60} \frac{1}{t+1} \left( \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1} \right) \,\text{d}t = \int_{0}^{60} \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{(t + 1)^2} \,\text{d}t $$

steps.title: Evaluate using a calculator
steps.content: Use the numerical integration feature of your GDC to evaluate this integral.

gdc.instructions:
In Run-Matrix mode, select OPTN > CALC > ∫dx.
Enter the expression (200 - 40e^(-x/4)(x+5)) / (x+1)^2.
Set lower bound 0 and upper bound 60.

ti84.instructions:
Press MATH > 9 (fnInt).
Enter the integrand: (200 - 40*e^(-X/4)*(X+5)) / (X+1)^2
Integrate with respect to X from 0 to 60.

desmos.instructions:
Type 'int' to create an integral symbol.
Set bounds 0 to 60.
Enter the integrand.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives a value of approximately $36.702...$ grams. Round this to 3 significant figures.

Amount of salt left $$\approx 36.7 \text{ grams}$$

steps.title: Method Strategy
steps.content:
To find the amount of salt that left the reservoir, we use the conservation principle (Input minus Accumulation). This states that the total amount of salt that leaves is equal to the total amount that entered minus the amount remaining in the reservoir at time $t=60$:

$$\text{Total Out} = \text{Total In} - \text{Amount Remaining}$$

steps.title: Calculate Salt Entered
steps.content:
The rate at which salt enters is the first term of the differential equation, $10\text{e}^{-t/4}$. We integrate this rate from $t=0$ to $t=60$ to find the total salt input:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total In} &= \int_{0}^{60} 10\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \,\text{d}t \\ &= \left[ -40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}} \right]_{0}^{60} \\ &= -40\text{e}^{-15} - (-40\text{e}^{0}) \\ &= 40(1 - \text{e}^{-15}) \\ &\approx 40 \text{ grams} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix, press OPTN > CALC > ∫dx. Enter ∫(10e^(-x/4), 0, 60)

calculator.description: Calculate the definite integral for salt input

steps.title: Find Salt Remaining x(60)
steps.content:
To find the salt remaining, $x(60)$, we solve the differential equation $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} + \frac{x}{t + 1} = 10\text{e}^{-t/4}$. Using the integrating factor $(t+1)$, the solution is:

$$x(t) = \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-\frac{t}{4}}(t + 5)}{t + 1}$$

Substituting $t=60$:

$$\begin{aligned} x(60) &= \frac{200 - 40\text{e}^{-15}(65)}{61} \\ &\approx \frac{200}{61} \\ &\approx 3.28 \text{ grams} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate Final Answer
steps.content:
Finally, subtract the amount remaining from the total amount entered:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Salt Left} &= \text{Total In} - x(60) \\ &= 40(1 - \text{e}^{-15}) - 3.28 \\ &= 40 - 3.28 \\ &= 36.7 \text{ grams} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Find the general solution using the integrating factor method.
parts.markscheme:
The equation is in the form $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+P(x) y=Q(x)$, with $P(x)=2, Q(x)=x e^{-x}$.

Integrating factor: $e^{\int 2 \mathrm{~d} x}=e^{2 x}$.
Multiplythroughbye ${ }^{2 x}$ :

$$
e^{2 x} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+2 e^{2 x} y=x e^{x} .(M 1)
$$

Left-hand side is $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}\left(y e^{2 x}\right)$.
Integrate:

$$
y e^{2 x}=\int x e^{x} \mathrm{~d} x .(M 1)
$$

Use integration by parts: let $u=x, \mathrm{~d} v=e^{x} \mathrm{~d} x$, so $\mathrm{d} u=\mathrm{d} x, v=e^{x}$ :

$$
\int x e^{x} \mathrm{~d} x 54=x e^{x}-\int e^{x} \mathrm{~d} x=x e^{x}-e^{x}+C .(A 1)
$$

Thus:

$$
y e^{2 x}=x e^{x}-e^{x}+C \Longrightarrow y=(x-1) e^{-x}+C e^{-2 x} \cdot(A 1)
$$

[7 marks]

parts.content: Determine the particular solution satisfying the initial condition.
parts.markscheme:
Use $x=0, y=0$ :

$$
0=(0-1) e^{0}+C e^{0} \Longrightarrow 0=-1+C \Longrightarrow C=1 .(M 1)(A 1)
$$

Particular solution:

$$
y=(x-1) e^{-x}+e^{-2 x} \cdot(A 1)
$$

[3 marks]

parts.content: Find the limit of $y$ as $x \rightarrow \infty$.
parts.markscheme:
As $x \rightarrow \infty, e^{-x} \rightarrow 0, e^{-2 x} \rightarrow 0$ :

$$
y=(x-1) e^{-x}+e^{-2 x} \rightarrow 0 .(M 1)(A 1)
$$

[2 marks]

parts.content: Solve the differential equation using the integrating factor method, giving your answer in the form $y=f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
Rewrite the equation:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x}\right) y=2 .(M 1)
$$

Integrating factor: $e^{\int-\frac{1}{x} \mathrm{~d} x}=e^{-\ln x}=\frac{1}{x}$.
Multiplythrough : $\frac{1}{x} \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-\frac{y}{x^{2}}=\frac{2}{x}$.
(M1) Left-hand side is $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$.
Integrate:

$$
\frac{y}{x}=\int \frac{2}{x} \mathrm{~d} x=2 \ln x+C .(A 1)
$$

Thus:

$$
y=2 x \ln x+C x .(A 1)
$$

Use $x=1, y=0$ :

$$
0=2 \cdot 1 \cdot \ln 1+C \cdot 1 \Longrightarrow C=0 .(M 1)
$$

Solution:

$$
y=2 x \ln x \cdot(A 1)
$$

[7 marks]

parts.content: Find the coordinates of the point where the solution curve intersects the line $y=x$.
parts.markscheme:
Set $y=x$ :

$$
x=2 x \ln x \Longrightarrow 1=2 \ln x \Longrightarrow \ln x=\frac{1}{2} \Longrightarrow x=e^{1 / 2} \cdot(M 1)(A 1)
$$

Coordinates:

$$
\left(e^{1 / 2}, e^{1 / 2}\right) \cdot(A 1)
$$

[3 marks]

parts.content: Determine whether the solution curve has any points of inflection.
parts.markscheme:
Compute second derivative:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 \ln x+2, \quad \frac{\mathrm{~d}^{2} y}{\mathrm{~d} x^{2}}=\frac{2}{x} \cdot(M 1)(A 1)
$$

Since $x>0, \frac{\mathrm{~d}^{2} y}{\mathrm{~d} x^{2}}>0$, so no points of inflection.
[4 marks]

parts.content: Determine the particular solution satisfying the initial condition.
parts.markscheme:
Use $x=0, y=0$ :

$$
0=(0-1) e^{0}+C e^{0} \Longrightarrow 0=-1+C \Longrightarrow C=1
$$
<marks> M1 </marks> <marks> A1 </marks>

Particular solution:

$$
y=(x-1) e^{-x}+e^{-2 x} 
$$
 <marks> A1 </marks>

As $x \rightarrow \infty, e^{-x} \rightarrow 0, e^{-2 x} \rightarrow 0$ :

$$
y=(x-1) e^{-x}+e^{-2 x} \rightarrow 0 
$$

<marks> M1 </marks> <marks> A1 </marks>

questions.specification:
Consider the differential equation
\[
\frac{dy}{dx} + y = 2xe^{-x}, \text{given } y=1 \text{ when } x=0.
\]
This is a linear differential equation of the form $\frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x)$.

parts.content: Determine the integrating factor $I(x) = e^{\int P(x) \, dx}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the function $P(x) = 1$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the integrating factor: $I(x) = e^{\int 1 \, dx} = e^x$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify P(x)
steps.content:
To solve this linear differential equation, we first compare the given equation to the standard form provided in the question specification:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x)$$

The given equation is:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} + 1 \cdot y = 2xe^{-x}$$

By comparing the coefficient of $y$ in both equations, we can identify the function $P(x)$:

$$P(x) = 1$$

highlights.text: This is a linear differential equation of the form $\frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x)$

steps.title: Apply integrating factor formula
steps.content:
Next, we substitute $P(x) = 1$ into the integrating factor formula:

$$I(x) = e^{\int P(x) \, dx}$$

Substituting our value:

$$I(x) = e^{\int 1 \, dx}$$

highlights.text: Determine the integrating factor $I(x) = e^{\int P(x) \, dx}$

steps.title: Evaluate the integral
steps.content:
We evaluate the integral in the exponent. Using the standard integral $\int 1 \, dx = x$:

$$I(x) = e^x$$

Note: We do not include the constant of integration ($+C$) here, as it is not necessary for the integrating factor method.

parts.content: Hence, find the exact solution $y = f(x)$ to the differential equation.
parts.markscheme:
- Multiply the differential equation by $e^x$: $e^x \frac{dy}{dx} + e^x y = 2x$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Recognize the LHS as the derivative of a product: $\frac{d}{dx}(y e^x) = 2x$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Integrate both sides with respect to $x$: $y e^x = \int 2x \, dx \implies y e^x = x^2 + C$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Apply the initial condition ($x=0, y=1$): $1 \cdot e^0 = 0 + C \implies C = 1$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute $C=1$ and solve for $y$: $y = (x^2 + 1)e^{-x}$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

6 marks total

steps.title: Find the integrating factor
steps.content:
The differential equation is given in the standard linear form:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x)$$

Comparing this to our equation $\frac{dy}{dx} + 1y = 2xe^{-x}$, we identify $P(x) = 1$. The integrating factor $I(x)$ is calculated as:

$$I(x) = e^{\int P(x) \, dx} = e^{\int 1 \, dx} = e^x$$

steps.title: Multiply by integrating factor
steps.content:
Multiply the entire differential equation by the integrating factor $e^x$. This step prepares the left-hand side to be written as a product rule derivative.

$$e^x \frac{dy}{dx} + y e^x = 2xe^{-x} \cdot e^x$$

Simplifying the right-hand side using exponent laws ($e^{-x} \cdot e^x = e^0 = 1$):

$$e^x \frac{dy}{dx} + y e^x = 2x$$

steps.title: Rewrite LHS as product derivative
steps.content:
We can now recognize the left-hand side as the derivative of the product $y \cdot e^x$. This is the key step of the Integrating Factor Method.

$$\frac{d}{dx}(y e^x) = 2x$$

steps.title: Integrate both sides
steps.content:
Integrate both sides with respect to $x$ to remove the derivative.

$$\begin{aligned} \int \frac{d}{dx}(y e^x) \, dx &= \int 2x \, dx \\ y e^x &= x^2 + C \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the constant C
steps.content:
Substitute the given initial condition $x=0, y=1$ into the equation to solve for the constant of integration $C$.

$$\begin{aligned} (1)e^0 &= (0)^2 + C \\ 1 \cdot 1 &= 0 + C \\ C &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final solution
steps.content:
Substitute $C=1$ back into the general solution and rearrange to solve for $y$.

$$\begin{aligned} y e^x &= x^2 + 1 \\ y &= \frac{x^2 + 1}{e^x} \\ y &= (x^2 + 1)e^{-x} \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Find the $x$-coordinates of the points of inflection of the graph of $y = f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Find the first derivative:
$$
y' = 2xe^{-x} - (x^2+1)e^{-x} = e^{-x}(2x - x^2 - 1) = -e^{-x}(x-1)^2
$$
<marks>M1</marks>
- Find the second derivative using the product rule:
$$
y'' = e^{-x}(x-1)^2 + (-e^{-x})(2(x-1))
$$
<marks>M1</marks>
- Factorize the expression: $y'' = e^{-x}(x-1)[(x-1) - 2] = e^{-x}(x-1)(x-3)$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Set $y'' = 0$ to identify potential inflection points: $x=1$ or $x=3$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State the $x$-coordinates: $x = 1, x = 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

<note>At $x=1$, $y' = 0$ (stationary inflection point). At $x=3$, $y' \neq 0$ (non-stationary inflection point). Both are valid inflection points.</note>

steps.title: Solve the differential equation
steps.content:
First, we need to find the function $y(x)$ by solving the given linear differential equation. We use the integrating factor method ($$I(x) = e^{\int P(x) dx}$$).

The equation is $$\frac{dy}{dx} + y = 2xe^{-x}$$, so $$P(x) = 1$$.

Integrating factor:
$$I(x) = e^{\int 1 \, dx} = e^x$$

Multiply the DE by $$e^x$$:
$$e^x \frac{dy}{dx} + e^x y = 2x$$
$$\frac{d}{dx}(ye^x) = 2x$$

Integrate both sides:
$$ye^x = \int 2x \, dx = x^2 + C$$
$$y = (x^2 + C)e^{-x}$$

steps.title: Apply initial condition
steps.content:
Use the given initial condition to find the constant $$C$$.

Substitute $$x=0, y=1$$:
$$1 = (0^2 + C)e^0$$
$$1 = C$$

So the function is:
$$y = (x^2 + 1)e^{-x}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate (0^2 + 1) * e^0 to verify it equals 1

steps.title: Find the first derivative
steps.content:
To find points of inflection, we first need $$y'$$. Apply the product rule to $$y = (x^2 + 1)e^{-x}$$.

Using the product rule formula $$(uv)' = uv' + vu'$$:
Let $$u = x^2 + 1 \Rightarrow u' = 2x$$
Let $$v = e^{-x} \Rightarrow v' = -e^{-x}$$

$$y' = (x^2 + 1)(-e^{-x}) + e^{-x}(2x)$$
$$y' = e^{-x}(-(x^2 + 1) + 2x)$$
$$y' = e^{-x}(-x^2 + 2x - 1)$$

Factor out the negative and recognizing the perfect square:
$$y' = -e^{-x}(x^2 - 2x + 1)$$
$$y' = -e^{-x}(x-1)^2$$

steps.title: Find the second derivative
steps.content:
Now differentiate $$y'$$ to find $$y''$$. Apply the product rule again to $$y' = -e^{-x}(x-1)^2$$.

Let $$u = -e^{-x} \Rightarrow u' = e^{-x}$$
Let $$v = (x-1)^2 \Rightarrow v' = 2(x-1)$$

$$y'' = (-e^{-x})(2(x-1)) + (x-1)^2(e^{-x})$$

Factor out $$e^{-x}(x-1)$$:
$$y'' = e^{-x}(x-1)[-2 + (x-1)]$$
$$y'' = e^{-x}(x-1)(x - 3)$$

steps.title: Solve for inflection points
steps.content:
Points of inflection occur where $$y'' = 0$$ (and the concavity changes).

Set $$y'' = 0$$:
$$e^{-x}(x-1)(x-3) = 0$$

Since $$e^{-x} > 0$$ for all $$x$$, we solve:
$$(x-1)(x-3) = 0$$

This gives two solutions:
$$x = 1$$ or $$x = 3$$

Both are valid points of inflection as the sign of $$y''$$ changes at these values.

gdc.instructions: Graph y = e^(-x)(x-1)(x-3) and find roots

ti84.instructions: Graph Y1 = e^(-X)*(X-1)*(X-3) and find zeros

desmos.instructions: Graph y = e^{-x}(x-1)(x-3) and look for x-intercepts

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The $$x$$-coordinates of the points of inflection are:

$$x = 1, \quad x = 3$$

steps.title: Solve the differential equation
steps.content:
First, we need the explicit solution $y(x)$ to substitute later. The given equation is a **linear differential equation** of the form $\frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x)$ with $P(x) = 1$. 

We calculate the integrating factor $I(x)$:
$$I(x) = e^{\int 1 \, dx} = e^x$$

Multiply the differential equation by $e^x$:
$$e^x\frac{dy}{dx} + e^xy = 2xe^{-x}e^x$$
$$\frac{d}{dx}(ye^x) = 2x$$

Integrate both sides with respect to $x$:
$$ye^x = \int 2x \, dx = x^2 + C$$

Using the initial condition $y=1$ when $x=0$:
$$1 \cdot e^0 = 0^2 + C \implies C = 1$$

So, the solution is:
$$y = (x^2 + 1)e^{-x}$$

steps.title: Differentiate the differential equation
steps.content:
Using the **Differentiating the Differential Equation** approach, we differentiate the given equation implicitly with respect to $x$. This gives us a relationship involving $y''$.

Given equation:
$$\frac{dy}{dx} + y = 2xe^{-x}$$

Differentiating with respect to $x$ (using the product rule on the RHS):
$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right) + \frac{d}{dx}(y) = \frac{d}{dx}(2xe^{-x})$$

$$y'' + y' = 2(e^{-x} + x(-e^{-x}))$$

$$y'' + y' = 2e^{-x}(1 - x)$$

steps.title: Apply condition for inflection points
steps.content:
Points of inflection occur where the concavity changes, which corresponds to $y'' = 0$. Substitute $y'' = 0$ into our differentiated equation from Step 1:

$$0 + y' = 2e^{-x}(1 - x)$$
$$y' = 2e^{-x}(1 - x)$$

Now, substitute this expression for $y'$ back into the **original** differential equation $\frac{dy}{dx} + y = 2xe^{-x}$ to find $y$ in terms of $x$ at the inflection points:

$$(2e^{-x}(1 - x)) + y = 2xe^{-x}$$

Solving for $y$:
$$y = 2xe^{-x} - 2e^{-x}(1 - x)$$
$$y = 2xe^{-x} - 2e^{-x} + 2xe^{-x}$$
$$y = 4xe^{-x} - 2e^{-x} = 2e^{-x}(2x - 1)$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
We now have two expressions for $y$:
1. The general solution: $y = (x^2 + 1)e^{-x}$ (from Step 0)
2. The condition at inflection points: $y = (4x - 2)e^{-x}$ (from Step 2)

Equating these gives:
$$(x^2 + 1)e^{-x} = (4x - 2)e^{-x}$$

Since $e^{-x} \neq 0$, we can divide both sides by it:
$$x^2 + 1 = 4x - 2$$
$$x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0$$

Factorizing the quadratic:
$$(x - 3)(x - 1) = 0$$

Therefore, the $x$-coordinates are:
$$x = 1 \quad \text{or} \quad x = 3$$

steps.title: Confirm findings
steps.content:
The points of inflection occur at $x=1$ and $x=3$. We can verify that $y''$ changes sign at these points using the factored form from the markscheme $y'' = e^{-x}(x-1)(x-3)$.
- For $x < 1$, $y'' > 0$ (Concave up)
- For $1 < x < 3$, $y'' < 0$ (Concave down)
- For $x > 3$, $y'' > 0$ (Concave up)

Thus, both are valid points of inflection.

questions.specification:
A population $P$ of bacteria grows according to the differential equation

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d} P}{\mathrm{~d} t}=\frac{P(1000-P)}{1000}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

where $t$ is time in hours and $P$ is the population size. At $t=0, P=200$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > AHL 5.18—1st order DE’s – Euler method, variables separable, integrating factor, homogeneous DE using sub y=vx > Question Type 2: Solving separable differential equations
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Solving separable differential equations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The model dP/dt = P(1000-P)/1000 is a first-order separable (logistic) differential equation; part (a) requires solving it by separation with an initial condition, and part (c) uses the explicit solution to solve for t when P=500.

parts.content: Solve the differential equation to find $P$ as a function of $t$.
parts.markscheme:
Rewrite:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d} P}{\mathrm{~d} t}=\frac{P(1000-P)}{1000} .(A 1)
$$

Separate variables:

$$
\int \frac{1000}{P(1000-P)} \mathrm{d} P=\int \mathrm{d} t .(M 1)
$$

Partial fractions:

$$
\frac{1000}{P(1000-P)}=\frac{A}{P}+\frac{B}{1000-P} .(M 1)
$$

Solve: $1000=A(1000-P)+B P$, so $A=1, B=1$.
Thus:

$$
\int\left(\frac{1}{P}+\frac{1}{1000-P}\right) \mathrm{d} P=\int \mathrm{d} t .(A 1)
$$

Integrate:

$$
\ln P-\ln (1000-P)=t+C \Longrightarrow \ln \left(\frac{P}{1000-P}\right)=t+C .(A 1)
$$

Use $t=0, P=200$ :

$$
\ln \left(\frac{200}{800}\right)=\ln \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)=C .(M 1)
$$

Thus:

$$
\ln \left(\frac{P}{1000-P}\right)=t+\ln \left(\frac{1}{4}\right) .
$$

Solve:

$$
\frac{P}{1000-P}=\frac{e^{t}}{4} \Longrightarrow P=\frac{1000 e^{t}}{4+e^{t}} .(A 1)
$$

[8 marks]

parts.content: Sketch the solution curve, indicating the behavior as $t \rightarrow \infty$.
parts.markscheme:
## $P_{\uparrow}-\cdots-\cdots \quad P=1000$

Asymptote $P=1000$ shown.
Increasing concave up then concave down behavior.
[3 marks]

parts.content: Find the time $t$ when the population reaches 500 , correct to two decimal places.
parts.markscheme:
Set $P=500$ :

$$
\frac{1000 e^{t}}{4+e^{t}}=500 \Longrightarrow e^{t}=4 .(M 1)(A 1)
$$

Solve:

$$
t=\ln 4 \approx 1.386 \approx 1.39 .(A 1)(A 1)
$$

[4 marks]
